---
title: "The Shell Game: Conceptual Framework"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{The Shell Game: Conceptual Framework}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment  = "#>"
)
```

## The Problem

Geographic crosswalking is commonplace in population data workflows. It is often described as
the harmonization of datasets across boundary systems. In practice, crosswalks allocate
observations between administrative geographies, altering the analytic sample while preserving
the variable name.

Crosswalking is not harmonization. It is allocation.

An observed quantity — population, cases, funding, rates — measured within boundary A is
redistributed into boundary B using a weighting rule. That redistribution is a model. It encodes
assumptions about proportionality, spatial distribution, and membership. It is not a new
observation.

The failure occurs when allocated outputs are treated as direct measurements.

An estimated or observed aggregate becomes an allocated value under a new boundary. The
workflow then proceeds as if that value were measured in the new geography, without
acknowledging that the estimand has changed, the allocation introduces additional uncertainty,
and the error structure is spatially patterned rather than random.

Crosswalking converts measurement into imputation. Treating the result as measurement
launders uncertainty.

**That is the shell game.**

---

## Estimand Drift

The underlying population does not change when boundaries change. What changes is the
question being answered.

"Population in ZIP 53704" is not the same statistical object as "population in ZCTA X," even if
they overlap heavily. The mapping alters the estimand. The resulting value is conditional on the
allocation rule. When that conditional quantity is treated as a direct observation, description is conflated with inference.

This problem does not require regression modeling to matter.

Descriptive statistics drive rankings, thresholds, and policy eligibility. When crosswalked values
are treated as fixed and error-free, confidence is overstated, boundary-driven artifacts appear
substantive, and threshold crossings may reflect allocation mechanics rather than population
reality. Variance is not eliminated by administrative transformation. It is obscured.

---

## Structured Uncertainty

Crosswalk error is not spatially uniform. It is largest where boundaries mismatch most: dense
urban cores, fragmented jurisdictions, annexation seams, historically segregated geographies.
These are frequently the same areas where marginalized populations reside.

When allocation-induced perturbation is ignored, the communities most vulnerable to boundary
distortion are also the ones most likely to experience mismeasurement. This is not random
noise. It is structured uncertainty.

Data plumbing is rarely audited. Analysts see valid code operating on numbers. What is not
visible is that the numbers themselves embody structural assumptions about geography and
proportionality. The shell game is not malicious. It is procedural. But once allocated values are
treated as observed parameters, the workflow silently shifts from measurement to modeled
estimation without acknowledging the transition.

That shift deserves explicit diagnosis.

---

## The Two Hidden Decisions

Every crosswalk workflow encodes at least two decisions that are typically left implicit.

**Decision Point 1: Membership Definition.** Analysts must choose whether to define geographic
membership by administrative linkage (relationship-based, as used in Census tabulations) or by
geometric contact (spatial intersection). In Hennepin County, Minnesota, this choice affects
whether 74 ZCTAs (relationship-based) or 94 ZCTAs (geometric intersection) constitute the
baseline — before any transformation occurs.

The relationship file reflects Census tabulation logic, not raw polygon contact. Geometric
intersection performs a literal spatial test: if a ZCTA polygon touches the county boundary, it
counts, including edge-touching, slivers, water boundaries, and TIGER topology artifacts. This
decision alone alters the baseline by 27% before any crosswalk is applied.

**Decision Point 2: Crosswalk Selection.** When allocating ZIP-level data to counties, analysts must
select among allocation ratios. The HUD ZIP–County crosswalk provides TOT_RATIO (total
addresses), RES_RATIO (residential addresses only), BUS_RATIO (business addresses), and
OTH_RATIO (other addresses). Each ratio embodies different assumptions about how phenomena
distribute across addresses — assumptions that may not hold uniformly across urban, suburban,
and rural contexts.

The analyst who selects TOT_RATIO assumes total addresses are a proxy for population
distribution. The analyst who selects RES_RATIO assumes residential addresses are more
appropriate. These are not technical details. They are methodological choices that affect
results.

---

## Quantifying Perturbation

The framework quantifies Δ*X*(VAR), defined as the change in an analytic variable induced
solely by geographic transformation and allocation choices, holding the underlying data source
constant:

> Δ*X*(VAR) = VAR₁ − VAR₀

Where VAR₀ is the baseline value at geography A, and VAR₁ is the post-transformation value.

Δ*X*(VAR) does not imply which representation is correct, nor that zero delta is inherently
desirable. It states only that the variable is not invariant under this transformation. A Δ*X* of
zero indicates that the variable survived transformation unchanged. A non-zero Δ*X* indicates
perturbation. The magnitude and direction of that perturbation are empirical questions, not
theoretical ideals.

---

## The Hennepin County Demonstration

Using Hennepin County, Minnesota (FIPS 27053) as the demonstration case, baseline data were
drawn from the 2022 ACS 5-year estimates at the ZCTA level (n = 74, variable B01001_001,
total population). The transformation pipeline followed two hops:

**Hop 1 — ZCTA → ZIP.** The first hop increased spatial units by 32.4%, from 74 ZCTAs to 98
ZIP codes, prior to any allocation or weighting. Some ZCTAs mapped to multiple ZIP codes:
ZCTA 55401 was associated with 8 ZIP codes, ZCTA 55402 with 6. This pre-allocation expansion
is the first measurable consequence of the transformation — the analytical surface has already
shifted before a single weight is applied.

**Hop 2 — ZIP → County.** Applying HUD TOT_RATIO weighting, the recovered value for
Hennepin County was 1,216,874 against a baseline of 1,391,557.

> Δ*X*(population) = −174,683 (−12.6%)

The 174,683 units not retained within Hennepin County were redistributed to adjacent counties
according to the HUD crosswalk weights. This perturbation is a property of the transformation
structure, not the data being transformed. The same pipeline applied to median household
income, total housing units, or households by vehicles available yields perturbation driven by
the same allocation mechanics, though magnitude varies by the distributional properties of the
variable.

---

## Why This Is Agnostic

The shell game occurs regardless of which variable is being analyzed, which tool is used to run
the workflow, or which geography is selected. Population, median income, vehicle ownership —
same percentage error structure. R, Python, Stata, ArcGIS — same result. Hennepin County or
any other — same pattern.

The transformation is the cause. Not the tool. Not the variable.

A crosswalk designed for population allocation may be inappropriate for income, employment,
or housing variables, yet analysts routinely apply the same crosswalk across diverse analytic
contexts. The choice of crosswalk is not a technical detail that can be deferred — it is a
methodological decision that determines what the output actually represents.

---

## Auditing the Audit

The framework was tested against a second crosswalk to verify that it reports honestly in both
directions. Applying an IPUMS NHGIS block-to-tract crosswalk to Census Tract 1016, Hennepin
County, using ACS 2019–2023 block group data returned:

> Δ*X*(population) = 0 (0.0%)

Three block groups intersect Tract 1016. Each maps entirely into the tract — no fragmentation,
no spillover across boundaries. Every block in every block group allocated at weight 1.0. The
variable survived the transformation unchanged.

This result is worth being precise about. A zero Δ*X* does not mean the crosswalk is correct,
or that the allocation assumptions have been externally validated, or that NHGIS resolves the
problem this framework describes. What it means is that for this geography, this crosswalk, and
this variable, the transformation introduced no perturbation. The boundary geometry and the
crosswalk logic happened to be well-matched.

IPUMS NHGIS applies its allocation logic rigorously and documents it carefully. That internal
consistency is genuinely valuable. It is not the same as epistemic completeness. The
assumptions are better labeled, not absent. A well-documented crosswalk that is mismatched
to the analytic context will still perturb the variable. The audit framework does not adjudicate
which crosswalk is appropriate — it measures what any given crosswalk actually does.

The zero result demonstrates three things: the math is correct, crosswalk fit to geography
determines the outcome, and the framework reports honestly when the answer is zero.

For the full script and results, see
`vignette("audit_the_audit", package = "geoDeltaAudit")`.

---

## What These Packages Do

`shellgame` formalizes the conceptual framework and audit protocol for documenting crosswalk
assumptions and evaluating how allocation alters the analytic sample. `geoDeltaAudit`
operationalizes that framework, quantifying Δ*X*(VAR) across allocation steps and making
crosswalk-induced sample change measurable.

Geographic crosswalks should be treated as allocation procedures. The outputs they produce
are conditional on the assumptions they encode. `shellgame` and `geoDeltaAudit` make those
assumptions visible, so that analysts can document how crosswalk choices shape the results
used for funding, resource allocation, and policy implementation.

For the full worked example, see `vignette("audit_walkthrough", package = "geoDeltaAudit")`.
